intentional_test_features: Tachycardia-is-a-sign-not-a-diagnosis discrimination: the AI Doctor must recognise that a sustained fast heart rate is a physical sign requiring a cause, and distinguish an appropriate (compensatory) sinus tachycardia from a reversible driver (heavy caffeine/energy-drink loading, acute stress and anxiety) versus a primary arrhythmia

Red herrings that lure toward the wrong answer: a fine tremor plus tachycardia tempts a thyrotoxicosis anchor, and atypical chest discomfort plus anxiety can be misread as an acute coronary or panic picture - the AI Doctor should weigh these against the strong reversible-stimulant history and the absence of weight loss/goitre/severe pain

Rate-and-rhythm reasoning: a regular rhythm at ~120-128 bpm with gradual onset alongside stimulant intake favours sinus tachycardia over the abrupt-onset, faster (150-220 bpm) re-entrant SVT and over the irregularly-irregular pulse of atrial fibrillation/flutter

Haemodynamic-stability assessment: the patient is alert, conversing in full sentences with no severe pain and no syncope, indicating a stable presentation appropriate for reassurance plus trigger removal rather than emergency treatment

Telehealth limits: rhythm cannot be confirmed as sinus over chat, and thyroid status cannot be excluded remotely - the AI Doctor should coach trigger cessation and a vagal/slow-breathing manoeuvre while arranging same-day in-person confirmation of sinus rhythm and routine TSH, escalating if instability develops

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-03)
